title: SplitTopologicalOrder
lean_code_with_comments:
def splOrder (lt : Node → Node → Prop) :
    SplitNode Node → SplitNode Node → Prop
  | .unsplit u, .unsplit v => lt u v
  | .unsplit u, .copy0 w   => lt u w
  | .unsplit u, .copy1 w   => lt u w ∨ u = w
  | .copy0 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u ∨ v = u
  | .copy0 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  | .copy0 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w ∨ v = w
  | .copy1 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u
  | .copy1 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  | .copy1 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w

theorem splAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic
-- claim_3_6 -- end statement
  := by
  have hAcyclic : G.IsAcyclic := hCADMG
  obtain ⟨lt, hlt⟩ := (acyclic_iff_topological_order G).mp hAcyclic
  exact (acyclic_iff_topological_order (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW)).mpr
    ⟨splOrder lt, aux_splTopologicalOrder G W hW lt hlt⟩

theorem splTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    (lt : Node → Node → Prop) (hlt : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splOrder lt)
-- claim_3_6 -- end statement
  := by
  -- `hCADMG` is carried in the signature for LN-faithfulness (the LN reads
  -- "For a CADMG `G`") but is not consumed in this proof: the
  -- topological-order construction of sub-claim~(b) only uses that the
  -- supplied `lt` *is* a topological order, not acyclicity of `G` directly.
  -- See the design-choice block above for the LN-faithfulness rationale.
  let _ := hCADMG
  exact aux_splTopologicalOrder G W hW lt hlt
lean_code_without_comments:
def splOrder (lt : Node → Node → Prop) :
    SplitNode Node → SplitNode Node → Prop
  | .unsplit u, .unsplit v => lt u v
  | .unsplit u, .copy0 w   => lt u w
  | .unsplit u, .copy1 w   => lt u w ∨ u = w
  | .copy0 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u ∨ v = u
  | .copy0 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  | .copy0 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w ∨ v = w
  | .copy1 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u
  | .copy1 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  | .copy1 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w

theorem splAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic

  := by
  have hAcyclic : G.IsAcyclic := hCADMG
  obtain ⟨lt, hlt⟩ := (acyclic_iff_topological_order G).mp hAcyclic
  exact (acyclic_iff_topological_order (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW)).mpr
    ⟨splOrder lt, aux_splTopologicalOrder G W hW lt hlt⟩

theorem splTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    (lt : Node → Node → Prop) (hlt : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splOrder lt)

  := by

  let _ := hCADMG
  exact aux_splTopologicalOrder G W hW lt hlt
lean_explanation:
The remark `claim_3_6` (`SplitTopologicalOrder`) bundles two sub-claims about the node-split graph $G_{\spl(W)}$: (a) it is acyclic, and (b) any topological order $<$ of $G$ lifts to an explicit topological order $<_{\spl}$ of $G_{\spl(W)}$. The Lean encoding splits this into three declarations on top of an implicit ambient carrier `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]` — the silent vertex type inherited verbatim from `def_3_1`, with decidable equality required because the row's signatures transit through `Finset`-backed graph operations.

`splOrder` is the Lean rendering of the LN's $<_{\spl}$. Its input `lt : Node → Node → Prop` is the LN's topological order $<$ on $J \cup V$, taken as a bare binary relation rather than a typeclass instance. The output is a relation `SplitNode Node → SplitNode Node → Prop`, where the carrier `SplitNode Node` is `def_3_11`'s three-constructor inductive — `unsplit`, `copy0`, `copy1` — realising the LN's tagged disjoint-union $J \cup (V \setminus W) \sqcup W^0 \sqcup W^1$. The definition is a nine-case pattern match over the two constructor arguments, reading off the lex order on (base node, copy tag) directly. Equivalence with the LN's $\mathbb{Q}$-valued index map $\phi$ is by case-by-case unwinding (see the design notes); the lex / case-analysis presentation is the more Lean-natural shape.

The two theorems share a common prefix of hypotheses. `G : CDMG Node` is the LN's CADMG $G$; `hCADMG : G.IsCADMG` is the named attribute from `def_3_7` (which unfolds to acyclicity plus a bidirected-edge constraint and so in particular gives `G.IsAcyclic`). `W : Finset Node` with `hW : W ⊆ G.V` is the LN's $W \subseteq V$, with `⊆` here the `Finset.subset` relation. The expression `G.nodeSplittingOn W hW` is the LN's $G_{\spl(W)}$, constructed by `def_3_11`. `splAcyclic` concludes `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic` (per `def_3_6`). `splTopologicalOrder` additionally takes `lt` and a hypothesis `hlt : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` (per `def_3_8`), and concludes that `splOrder lt` is itself a topological order of the split graph — `def_3_8`'s `IsTopologicalOrder` predicate is reused verbatim, polymorphic in the node type. Mathlib idioms in play: dot-notation receivers (`G.IsAcyclic`, `G.IsTopologicalOrder lt`) for readability, `Finset` subset and membership reasoning throughout, and `Prop`-valued binary relations in place of `LT` / `LinearOrder` typeclass machinery.
design_choices:
**Case-analysis encoding of `<_{\spl}`, not $\mathbb{Q}$-arithmetic.** The LN defines $<_{\spl}$ via an index map $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \mathbb{Q}$ assigning $j \pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ to the tagged copies and $j$ to unsplit nodes. We encode `splOrder` directly as a nine-case pattern match on the two `SplitNode` constructors. Three reasons. (i) The $\mathbb{Q}$ formulation reuses no Mathlib infrastructure: every proof step would need to thread through a custom $\mathrm{idx} : \mathrm{Node} \to \mathbb{N}$ axiom and $\mathbb{Q}$-coercions. (ii) The lex structure on (base node, copy tag) is structurally visible in the case analysis but hidden under the $\mathbb{Q}$-arithmetic surface. (iii) The case-analysis form sidesteps the subtlety that the LN's text leaves indices of unsplit nodes implicit — the first constructor pair makes "unsplit nodes compare via the underlying $<$" literal.

**`W` does not appear in `splOrder`'s signature.** A naive translation would parameterise `splOrder` by $W$, but the lex form depends only on `lt` and the constructor tags. The $W$-dependence is absorbed into the carrier `SplitNode Node` (each constructor is formally distinct regardless of $W$-membership), and the $W$-dependent membership facts live in the underlying CDMG `G.nodeSplittingOn W hW`. Threading $W$ through `splOrder` would force every call site to project it back out.

**Lex orientation $\mathrm{copy0} < \mathrm{unsplit} < \mathrm{copy1}$ is load-bearing.** It is forced by `def_3_11`'s edge conventions: the transfer edge added is $(w^0, w^1)$, every incoming edge onto a split node is reattached at the $\mathrm{copy0}$ tag, and every outgoing edge leaves the $\mathrm{copy1}$ tag. Parent-precedence in any topological order of the split graph therefore demands this orientation at each base node. Under the opposite tagging convention, the LN's symmetric $j \mp \tfrac{1}{3}$ index assignment would not produce a topological order — sub-claim (b) is correct as stated precisely because the index choice matches the tagging convention.

**Two separate theorems, not one bundled biconjunction.** The LN bundles (a) and (b) inside one $\mathrm{Rem}$, but the sub-claims have different downstream consumers: hard-intervention-then-node-split SWIG constructions need only acyclicity preservation. A bundled $\mathrm{splAcyclic} \land \mathrm{splTopologicalOrder}$-shaped theorem would force every consumer to take an unused topological-order argument or pattern-match through `.1` at every call site. Splitting also matches the chapter convention of one atomic predicate per sub-statement.

**`G.IsCADMG`, not `G.IsAcyclic`, as hypothesis.** The LN reads "For a CADMG $G$". Although `IsCADMG` unfolds to `IsAcyclic` plus a bidirected-edge constraint, taking the LN-named attribute keeps the Lean signature greppable from the LN without a translation step.

**`lt` as a bare `Node → Node → Prop`.** The LN universally quantifies over an arbitrary topological order. A `[LinearOrder Node]` / `[LT Node]` typeclass formulation would lock in a single canonical order per `Node` type, colliding with the LN's parameterisation by *any* topological order, and would force every consumer to manufacture an instance even when they only have a one-off witness from `claim_3_4`.
